title: Risotto con piselli
subtitle: Pea risotto with Parmesan and fresh mint
excerpt: A simple Venetian spring risotto with peas, Parmesan, and mint. Half the peas are blended into the stock to tint the rice green and build sweetness into the base; the rest go in whole near the end for texture. Finished with cold butter and a handful of fresh mint.
sourceNotes: Risi e bisi (rice and peas) is Venice's most emblematic dish, traditionally served on 25 April (St Mark's Day) and documented from the fourteenth century. The drier risotto version with peas is a contemporary variant of the same tradition. Artusi includes risi e bisi and related Venetian rice and vegetable preparations in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain). Frozen peas are an acceptable substitute for fresh. They are often sweeter than out-of-season fresh peas.

content.text: The split-pea technique transforms a plain pea risotto into something with real depth. Half the peas go into the stock: blended until smooth, they turn the cooking liquid bright green and build sweetness into every grain from the first ladle. The remaining peas go in whole near the end and stay distinct, giving the finished risotto two registers: the base sweetness of the blended peas and the fresh, slightly popped texture of the whole ones.

Frozen peas work well here and are often sweeter than out-of-season fresh. If using fresh peas, add the pods to the stock for the blending step. They carry flavour. Mint at the end is not optional; it lifts the sweetness and keeps the dish from feeling heavy.

items.name: Frozen peas
items.prepNote: or fresh shelled peas; use 200 g blended into stock, 200 g added whole

items.name: Unsalted butter
items.prepNote: 20 g for cooking, 40 g cold for mantecatura

content.text: Put 200 g of the {{peas-frozen}} in a small saucepan with the {{stock-vegetable}} of vegetable stock. Bring to a simmer for 3 minutes. Blend until smooth and strain back into the saucepan. Season lightly and keep hot. You should have roughly 1 litre of bright green stock.

Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and 20 g of the {{unsalted-butter}} in a wide, heavy pan over medium heat. Add the {{shallot}} shallots and cook for 5 minutes until soft and translucent. Add the {{arborio-rice}} of risotto rice and stir for 2 minutes until the grains look translucent at the edges.

Pour in the {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine. Stir until absorbed. Add the hot green stock a ladleful at a time, stirring constantly and waiting for each addition to absorb before the next. After 14 minutes, stir in the remaining 200 g of whole peas. Continue cooking for 4 more minutes until the rice is done and the peas are tender.
Remove from the heat. Add the remaining cold butter and the {{parmesan}} of Parmesan. Beat vigorously for 60 seconds: this is the
. The risotto should be glossy, green, and flowing. Add the {{mint}} leaves. Taste for salt and pepper. Cover and rest for 2 minutes before serving.

items.fix: Simmer the peas for stock only 3 minutes; add the whole peas only in the last 4 minutes of cooking
items.cause: Stock overcooked before blending, or whole peas added too early and turned grey
items.symptom: Risotto is grey rather than bright green

items.fix: Blend the full 200 g into the stock; season the green stock before ladling
items.cause: Too little peas blended into the stock, or stock not seasoned well

items.fix: Add whole peas only at 14 minutes into cooking; reduce heat slightly after they go in
items.cause: Added too early, or heat too high after adding them
items.symptom: Whole peas have burst and become mushy

items.fix: Add torn mint leaves only after the mantecatura, just before resting
items.cause: Mint added during cooking rather than at the very end

content.text: Risi e bisi (rice and peas, in a loose, soupy form between a risotto and a minestrone) is the signature dish of Venice. It was made for the doge on 25 April, the feast day of Saint Mark. The link between peas and spring celebration is ancient. The pea harvest in the Venetian lagoon was once so brief and prized that fresh peas were treated as a luxury and presented at court. The drier risotto version is a modern simplification, but it draws on the same seasonal instinct: a spring dish built around one ingredient at its best.
